speaker: interviewer
text: Aaron, you were talking about your mother and how cultured she was, and how she was responsible for you learning about literature. Do you want to continue about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was a-- she was a member of a library which would send books to her. And she would leave them so I could read them. And the name of the-- the exact translation was the library of the golden nuggets. And I would-- I was reading [INAUDIBLE] when I was 10 years old. I read when I was 11 years old. And before that, I read and and and-- and Karl May, and all this literature. I read Jack London. Every book there is to-- to be-- to be read about Jack London, I have read. And so she really, not that she pushed me, she just left it available for me. Until one day, I started reading [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she really put those ones, she really just left the ones that she wanted me to read-- [INAUDIBLE]. But that-- that was a good book, by the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] I remember reading that. I was 11 years old. I used to sneak it out and read it. But I owe it to her. And she with her-- with her sister, would take me, and really take me everywhere with them. I mean, I-- I saw plays at the theater, and in movies, and I-- it's amazing the detail that I can tell you about the movies that I saw 60 years ago, and 55 and 58 years ago. But ask me what I had to eat yesterday. But-- so, go back to my mother, she taught me all that. They took me to concerts. They took me to the ballet. An interesting thing was, when we got our radio, which was like when you got your-- your first television, on Wednesday night, she would get all our cousins, particularly the one that she was being like their mentor. I had several cousins that they're going through high school. And they would come study with her. And they would-- she would help her. And after that, one of the cousins repaid by teaching me. But anyway, there was a program by the on-- on the opera. It was a famous Bulgarian singer by the name of Mazarov. M-A-Z-A-R-O-V. And every Wednesday night, religiously, they would have it on-- Radio Sofia had very good classical programs, period. And that-- it was a shame, because then the minute the-- the fascists came, they-- they restricted-- they came and they sealed it. And they restricted only to one station. My father used to love to listen to France. And they restricted it. And after that, they confiscated the radio. Funny thing was that my father went and found that radio, after they confiscated it, and apropos of that, he also, during First World War, they confiscated his horse. Because the general liked the horse so much, he picked it out of all the horses. He got this horse. He confiscated it. And then after the war, my father hunted it that down. And the name of the horse was [? Altschul. ?] He found it back. So he found the radio, too. To give you a little bit, my father had tenacity. [LAUGHS] And so, speaking about-- it was an interesting background that I came from, between my mother, the getting the-- the culture from my mother, and getting the-- the strong ethic, business ethic from my father. Has been extremely helpful throughout my entire life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A kid brother. I call him my kid brother. But he was just 60 years old. September 2nd, he was 60 years old. Yes. I have a brother who is seven years younger than me-- almost seven years younger than me. And I left on my own and then I left him there. That they came to Israel. And I had been in Israel already five years. I was in a kibbutz. I was a member of a kibbutz. So when they came to Bulgaria, I plucked him out from my parents. Because they are put in a, like a detention camp, for a short time until they found their own dwelling. And Bulgarians-- just have to tell you a very interesting thing. They're very industrious. The Israeli government never did a thing for them because they didn't want to do anything. They were very self-sufficient. They are self-reliant. And as soon as they came to Israel, immediately they went through all sorts of-- of businesses and all sorts of work. They worked with their hands. They worked as craftsmen. And they went into professions. And lucky to say that my cousin is one of the most prominent physician in Israel, just retired. And I have other cousins that-- they're-- they're all physicians. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about your brother and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: About my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your childhood. Let's-- let's stick to that time frame and try to draw a picture of what was it like living in Bulgaria with your brother as a child before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came from Gorna Dzhumaya, I was about 4 and 1/2 years old. We came to Plovdiv and resided in a beautiful place, like, downstairs, complete apartment. And I have to tell you, with a beautiful garden in the front. And above us were a retired Bulgarian couple, merchants. Very prominent people. Their daughter was married to the ambassador of Bulgaria to Germany. Just to give you that they were really-- extremely-- tell you how wealthy they were. If you can imagine yourself 60 years ago, 62 years ago, in Bulgaria, on the second floor, having hot shower with a bathtub-- complete bathroom. And I remember, because I was five years old, my mother would send me up. And they-- they had grown children, and they had no grandchildren around them, because they were in Germany. And she like, sort of adopted me. And whenever there was, like once a week, my mother would send me up there, and she'd take me, she-- I'll have a bath right there with her. And the-- her husband was in-- in those days, in his late 60s. He would walk around the garden. He would look up and say, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], come out here. Let me look at you. You're so beautiful. She was of Greek origin. And he was Bulgarian. And every so often, you know, he would knock down. That means for me to go up and have lunch with them or dinner with them. So it was a wonderful childhood in those days. And then, at the age of seven, my mother was pregnant. And I can-- I can listen to the conversations that my aunts will have or my relatives will have with my-- my mother. They didn't think I was-- I would listen to that. And they would say, be very careful because he's been on his own seven years. And he has gotten so much attention. And when this youngest-- his younger, you know, sibling will be born, he may display some jealousy. He may hurt the baby. So be extremely careful. Nothing of the sort. From the day he was born, I loved that kid. And my mother had such a confidence in me that she would leave him with me. I was seven years old, eight years old. I would be babysitting him while she would go to the Turkish bath. You know. And so I-- I was always super protective with him. And then when the landlady's daughter came back from Germany, they had-- we had to leave that house. And we went to live in another house, of which I have a picture that just came back from Bulgaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, my brother, wherever I went, I always grabbed him with me. And one time, I remember that some kids picked on him. And I ran after that kid. And-- and he was just about to go into his house. So I took a large stick and I just threw that stick, hit him in-- in the legs. And he just fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I could hear the screams so I ran to stay with my-- my aunt for two days. I was afraid to come home. But I was always protective of my-- of my kid brother. But then when I-- when he was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, seven years old, then I left for Israel, from Bulgaria. So I did not see him for five years. And then, I was with him only two years. And then I married and I came to-- to-- to this country. So-- but when I was in-- in Israel, I made sure that he went and he studied in [NON-ENGLISH] and then in [NON-ENGLISH]. Those were very good schools. They work half a day, they study half a day. And I wanted him to study English and-- because my-- my wife was American. And she taught me how important it would be for them to study English, as well as you know that today in Israel, practically everybody speaks English. But he would not learn that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he just-- I realized, as my father had told me, that I would be the salesperson in the-- in the family, and I will be always make a living by selling and by-- by talking. He was a craftsman. He was three years old when my father decided that I was going to be the salesperson and he was going to be the craftsman. Let's go back to that time. So at--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a wonderful time. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that age, you-- you already had a sense as to who you were and he was already good with his hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. He was my kid brother. And like I says, we were-- and they have an expression in-- in-- in Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH], which means-- which means, underwear and your behind. I mean, we were inseparable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you enjoy doing together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would take him-- you know, one of the fun thing was, we lived right next to a hill. Because Plovdiv is located on several hills. And on top of that hill, they had, like, a recreation center. And there were-- there were places where you going to slide down, like slides. And I would take him with me all the time there, and we would slide. And we'd play soccer together. And in Bulgaria, it was very, very prominent, just like here, baseball, in Bulgaria, you played soccer every chance you had. Like, we break up in school, you know, and we'll be playing soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him with me. We played soccer. We played volleyball. When he was very little, still, we always involved with sports. Always involved together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it really hurt me to leave him. Because we were so, so close. And I was always super protective of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your mother went to a Turkish bath. Is this something that is traditionally Sephardic? How often did one go to a bath? And was this just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me Let me tell you, Cydney, there are no mikvahs in-- in-- in Bulgaria. Not to my knowledge, anyway. I don't remember in Plovdiv, or in Gorna Dzhumaya. Maybe they had it in Sofia. I'm not sure. But there were no mikvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So until the age of five or six, I would go with my mother. And then after I was certain age, of course, I would go with my father. But it was-- that was the ritual.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have showers inside the house. And we're lucky to have indoor, you know, facilities. So weekly or as often as we could, I would go with my father to the Turkish bath. My mother, on Fridays, would go to the Turkish bath. It was a ritual. And we had several of them in-- in Plovdiv. One of them was right in the middle of the Jewish neighborhood. And it was called, I remember, [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- but it was a ritual. And then the Bulgarians-- those baths were still from-- from Turkish time. And if you recall, Bulgaria was a crossroads as well as Turkey, for the Byzantine Empire, for the Romans. And baths, it was a ritual with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those baths were located in several parts of the city. So the-- the closest one. But when they built the new bath, the new Turkish bath, it was so elaborate, with steam and everything, that's the same concept. So I remember us start going to that bath. And-- and-- and once in a while, we'd go to the old Turkish baths. But we would go to the new Turkish, which is really Bulgarian bath, but was very modern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very modern, with mosaics and with tiles, and with this-- now, another ritual-- they would have big, husky men in the men's area and heavy-set ladies for the ladies areas, that They would wear something on their hands and there would be straps through their waist and from waist up, they'll be-- they'll be naked. And the men, we would go and those men were big, very strong. And after we were into the steam, they would come and they rub your skin to make sure that nothing unclean was left on the skin. And when you left there, you were clean. Let me tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to call them-- and the-- the word in there was [NON-ENGLISH]. So my kid brother and I would go with-- with our father, when he became a little older. So the men would go to-- to to the bath. It was a big ritual. So that's the way we kept ourselves clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not necessarily related to Sephardic Jews or to-- to-- to the Bulgarian Jews. That was the way-- but they incorporate into the Jewish life. Because most of the time we always went on a Friday, before the Sabbath. That was the ritual that we did. And it was, I recall, in fact, when we Istanbul, I wanted to go to one, but I just didn't-- didn't get a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Ashkenazis, did they also partake in-- in the baths? And were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: When you-- you know, when you say Ashkenazi, there were very few of those. And they're totally integrated. Like my best friend that became, after that, professor in one of the foremost authority on-- on the biblical studies, Hanoch Friedman, who came just little before me to-- to Israel with a different group, and then we were both together at the kibbutz. But his mother was my father's best-- my mother's best friend. Interesting thing about that family. My mother and his mother, god rest her soul, were classmates. Hanoch and I were classmates. [INAUDIBLE] with Amnon were classmates. So we went all these generations, classmates, at the same-- it was just by coincidence. But they came with us. Hanoch studied with me. And he was no different than a Sephardic Jew. We went to temple together. We went to the [INAUDIBLE] midrash together. We went everywhere together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever sense any conflict between the Ashkenazis and the Sephardics in Bulgaria at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They made little fun, you know, they used to call them [? Ashkenazi. ?] But not really, no. Absolutely not. No conflict. There was no difference. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the same synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same synagogue. There were no different synagogue. Within the same synagogue, the same schools. We went to the same ritual baths. We went to everything the same. Played together, fought together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mode of transportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you said something about memory. I was five years old. I get up one Sunday morning while my father was sleeping and my mother was busy in the kitchen. And there was some money on-- on the table. I took some money. And I was in my pajamas. And I got on the bus that stopped in front of the house. Start riding the bus. Five years old. Well, the-- the bus driver looked me. I don't even remember how it happened, but I-- I guess he realized that I did something wrong. But there were no phones.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my father followed on the next bus. All of a sudden, my mother is looking for me, and they realize. I was a very precocious child. But-- what was the question you asked me? The mode of transportation. You were on the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, mode of-- OK. We had buses. We had buses. We had very few taxis in those days. But primary mean of transportation was the horse-drawn carriage. And it was called [TURKISH], which is a Turkish word. I remember once I got very sick. And they had to take me to the hospital. And the first thing I request, it has to be a [TURKISH] drawn by white horses. So my father had to go and find the carriage with white horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a very, very big source. As I told you before, where my father would travel, that's the way they would come and pick him up in the middle of the night in-- in a horse-drawn carriage. And that was the biggest-- and then buses. And then-- then after that, they began to come with cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my-- my mother's cousins was the first one to have a car, actually chauffeur-driven car, because they were representative of a very large German-American company. So, primarily, was-- was horse-drawn carriage, buses, [DRINKS] and some cars. Oh. That was in the city. But otherwise, trains. I mean, that was a ritual. My father knew the train schedules. The train, the smallest part, if you had to go from here to-- to the smallest village, it was by train. They had a very good train system there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first signs that you felt of anti-Semitism in growing up during your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me just give you a little example of the street in which we lived, after we moved from original and we moved into the second dwelling, which in which I grew up for the following seven years. The landlady was Bulgarian, the landlords. And I played with their son. And they had a son and-- my age and two, two daughters. Underneath us there were Armenians. I played with their kids. In fact, they were very helpful in raising me. Across the street, there were Turkish people. So I played with them. And they were Bulgarian, Turkish, Jews, and Armenians. The first sign of anti-Semitism was in the neighborhood, when somebody began to call me, like, a dirty Jew. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was in shock. Why is it-- I did hear from my father, there were some displays of anti-Semitism in his-- in his work. But he was so tough that he always was able to handle himself with that. But it was not anything prevalent. However, as the Nazis begin to advance and the fascist government-- government came, you could begin to see the signs. They formed some organizations which were called [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] like legions, and the [NON-ENGLISH], which are the defense. They wore uniforms. They were like real fascists. Then they began, if we were wandering in the main street, they would beat up on us or they would call us dirty names. And it was the-- The first foremost sign to me was, I had joined the Boy Scouts, Bulgarian Boy Scouts. And we would be going on-- on one of those hills. And we would excel, we'd be studying the Morse code, with-- with flags. It was fascinating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the friend that took me there, a Bulgarian friend from the neighborhood, one day very quietly comes to me, says to me, you can't come and be part of us anymore. And I said, why not? I paid my dues.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you're Jewish. Jews are not allowed anymore to join the scouts. However, you have Hashomer Hatzair, which we had three factions in-- in-- in Plovdiv-- the Maccabees, the Hashomer Hatzair, and Betar. However, Hashomer Hatzair is like boy scouts. So I suggest that you go to them. Well, I didn't. I was belonging to the Maccabees anyway. But it really hurt me to the point I went home crying. And my mother had to comfort me and said, brace yourself. Tough times are coming. And I couldn't understand that. So all of a sudden, you begin to see all these signs. And I told you that was the next sign, was when we were in temple, and the Bedouins began to come. Now my father worked in a company which, the name of the company was [NON-ENGLISH] which means the-- the brothers Benjamin Levy. One of the brothers had just come back from Germany. And I'm in the store because one month, during the summer I would work in the store. I was only 10 years old, nine years old. And one month, I'll be going on vacation. They'll send me to a camp. And I could hear that my-- my-- my father's boss, who was childless, they didn't have any children, and I was very, very favorite of him-- he was supposed to bring me something from Germany, like a carry-on bag. And he didn't. And he was explaining to my father, those are the night-- he witnessed the night that was the Kristallnacht. It's when the Germans went all over and broke all the windows in Berlin. And he was there. And he said, I couldn't wait to get on the train. Lucky that I got here. We are in for bad times. Then after the fascists came, and they established a law in defense of the nation, we had, from 10 years old, we had to wear the Jewish star. Because my-- my father's boss participated and he was awarded a medal during First World War, he wore only a yellow button. He did not wear the star. That was the privilege they accorded him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you saw people with just a little yellow button, you knew there is-- there-- those were veterans or those were former military people. That's the-- the-- the-- the honor they accorded him. It began, really, to get tough. Do you remember when you first put on your star and how it affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a shock. I mean it was just, just-- it came after-- first the restrictions came, then came the stars. First the restrictions came where all Jews could not walk on the main street. They could not walk around the train station or the train stations. They could not walk next to military areas, where in Plovdiv, they used to have-- they couldn't walk any place except certain designated areas and at certain hours. And then they came, they came, the-- the-- the-- the Jewish star. I have pictures in which you see as a kid, I had the Jewish star. And this is when we began to get picked on all the time. And we would go into certain areas and they would throw stone at us-- stones at us. And we go through certain areas and they would be calling us dirty Jews. And it just, like-- it-- it-- it changed so dramatically. I'm not saying there was no anti-Semitism in Bulgaria. But in Bulgaria, the Jews were always treated better than anywhere else by comparison. Anywhere in Europe. When the Jewish people in Bulgaria, they were even helpful in writing the Constitution, the Bulgarian constitution, after they liberated themselves from the Turks. And they were granted equal rights.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the church was very prominent in showing their displeasure with that. And I just came back from Bulgaria and a very elder churchman was confiding me-- to me, said to me, I could not understand why would our people do that? Until today. He was a fine, fine churchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clergy was incredibly remarkable in Bulgaria. They were the Bulgarian Orthodox. And I was taken to those churches as a kid, with my Bulgarian friends, with my Bulgarian neighbors, with my Bulgarian landlady and landlords. And, like, it was nothing to it. And it was very beautifully conducted services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you go in with your star on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's when it stopped. No. No. I'm talking pre-star.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things changed drastically--

speaker: interviewee
text: Drastically.

speaker: interviewer
text: --once you started wearing your star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Drastically. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What affected you most as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all of a sudden it affect me to the point that you see that my father is out of a job. I couldn't believe that. My father can't travel. The first thing came that my father couldn't travel anymore. He was restricted to-- only to the city. Then, after that, they closed down the store. They closed down all the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it became extremely difficult. In one way, our father was able to make a living for us, first he stole some of our own artifacts. And whatever money we had, we start using it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he was also able to sell for my uncles, and the family, and other people. Because he was so well plugged into the Bulgarian community there, through his travels and through his contacts, that he was able to-- to sell, primarily artifacts and belongings to the Jews. And that's the way we existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did this underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He did not do that underground. Actually, he did it quite open. And there were certain Bulgarian people that I'll never forget, that I would go with him and carry some stuff to them. They, some of them took very, very big advantage of that. They would get it practically for nothing. But my father was always careful to make sure that that did not happen. They tried. But most of the people, surprisingly enough, they give very fair value for the artifacts and for the belongings. Because in a way, they felt, some of them felt guilty about it. But my father had to convince them, look. You're doing it as a-- you need it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we need to sustain ourselves. So he-- he was able to do that. And that's the way they-- they-- they made a living. And your mother, what was her attitude?

speaker: interviewee
text: Devastated. Absolutely devastated. She had so many friends with the Bulgarians. And most of them stayed pretty close. But there-- quite a few of them, all of a sudden, you saw that they began to disassociate themselves from us. And that was very, very difficult for my mother because she was such a social, socially active lady, you know? And she corresponded with people. She really-- it was very difficult on her. And those are the days when-- and then, all of a sudden, we begin to hear that the Nazis are advancing. They are advancing. And they were going through Czechoslovakia. They're going through Hungary. The King of Bulgaria-- a brilliant man-- he came from German origin. His father was full-blooded German, Ferdinand [NON-ENGLISH]. He was half-German. Educated in Germany. But he was a very, very devoted Bulgarian. And no matter what controversy may-- may be around him, he lost his life over two things. Primarily, one of them was saving the Jews, refusing to send the Jews to the camps. And the other is refusing to give the Germans Bulgarian-- Bulgarians to-- to fight on the Eastern Front.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to continue, pause now, and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [DRINKS]
